one_line_takeaway: A broad taxonomic survey mapping federated learning onto cyber-physical-system domains (healthcare, smart city, vehicular, cybersecurity), useful as an orientation and reference map; its security value is in cataloguing FL-CPS threats (poisoning, Byzantine, membership inference) and defenses rather than contributing any new method or evaluation.
key_finding: FL is applicable and beneficial across CPS domains, and the survey collates domain-specific evidence from cited implementations: e.g., edge-cloud FL for remote health monitoring reaching 95.45% test accuracy (a reported 7.49% gain over a generic model) [127]; a cyber-layer framework maintaining 99.4% model accuracy under 15% adversarial participation in compliant smart grids; encrypted FedAvg reaching 94.3% prediction accuracy in power-grid CPS; and differential privacy trading utility for privacy (epsilon=2.0 Gaussian noise on X-ray gradients yields ~80% diagnostic accuracy, with up to ~12% accuracy degradation for epsilon<1.0). The recurring cross-cutting message: FL's central challenges in CPS are non-IID data, device heterogeneity, communication cost, real-time/safety constraints, and robustness to poisoning/Byzantine adversaries, which remain open.
core_contribution: This is a survey (IEEE Communications Surveys & Tutorials), not a novel method or system. Its self-stated contribution (Sec. I.B) is to be the 'first comprehensive and systematic analysis of FL integration within CPS networks.' Concretely it: (1) surveys and taxonomizes federated-learning research across critical CPS application domains (smart healthcare/HCPS, smart cities, intelligent transportation/vehicular CPS, and core cybersecurity services such as attack detection, authentication, and intrusion detection); (2) provides an in-depth comparison of CPS vs. IoT to clarify how FL's applicability differs between the two paradigms; (3) proposes an integration taxonomy framework (Data/Model/Aggregation/Control/Verification layers) and detailed per-domain taxonomy tables classifying architecture, data characteristics, learning paradigm, contributions, and limitations of each cited work; and (4) identifies research gaps and future directions. The distinction from Related Work is explicit: Table I positions the paper against prior surveys [17]-[18] that covered FL generally, FL-for-IoT/IIoT [19][20], or UAV-enabled FL [21], arguing none had examined FL specifically for CPS services (data offloading, connectivity, localization, caching, attack detection, CPS privacy). The quantitative figures throughout are drawn from the cited primary works being surveyed, not from experiments the authors ran.
threat_model: As a survey the paper does not commit to a single threat model of its own, but the FL-CPS security literature it reviews centers on: model-poisoning / targeted false-data-injection attacks against the aggregation process (framework of [151] detects and removes them, using blockchain + fuzzified one-way hashes [157] for dataset-integrity protection); Byzantine (malicious/faulty worker) attacks against aggregation, which several surveyed schemes address with Byzantine-robust aggregation and blockchain validation (reported ~92% attack detection); membership-inference attacks against shared model updates, mitigated via differential privacy (e.g., Gaussian noise on gradient updates); and privacy leakage from raw-data transmission, which FL structurally reduces by keeping data local (reported ~97% reduction in data exposure vs. centralized). The surveyed cybersecurity-service applications also treat FL itself as a tool for defense: federated intrusion-detection systems for HCPS [158] and attack detection for CPS. The paper does not propose or empirically evaluate a defense of its own.
limitations: (1) No original experiments or independent verification: every quantitative figure is inherited from a cited work, and the survey often presents them as bare percentages (e.g., '94.7% prediction accuracy across 50-node clusters', 'reduces collision false positives by 41%') without stating the dataset, baseline, or evaluation protocol, so provenance is hard to audit from the survey alone. (2) Some claims read as illustrative synthesis (e.g., specific weighting alpha=0.7 for healthcare CPS, layer-taxonomy naming UPPAAL/TLA+/ROS) rather than sourced measurements, blurring survey vs. authorial assertion. (3) Coverage breadth (healthcare, smart city, vehicular, cybersecurity) comes at the cost of depth on FL attack/defense mechanics; formal treatment of poisoning/inference threats is thin relative to the applications catalog. (4) As a 2025 arXiv preprint (cs.LG, 8 May 2025) it is a snapshot survey with the usual recency-decay limitation.

datasets_used: BraTS brain-tumor segmentation dataset [160] (FL reached ~99.1% accuracy, comparable to whole-dataset training)
Kaggle 'Brain MRI Segmentation' dataset split across 5 client sites (low-grade glioma segmentation)
Multi-site neuro MRI cohort: 181 MCI, 234 Alzheimer's, 232 Parkinson's, 208 severe MCI, 455 healthy subjects
TrashNet (MobileNetV2 feature extraction on IoT bins, ~89% accuracy)
Multi-institution COVID-19 chest X-ray + labs + vitals from 20 institutions (EXAM model [152])
quantitative_results: Edge-cloud FL remote health monitoring [127]: 95.45% test accuracy, reported +7.49% over a generic model
Cyber-layer framework in compliant smart grids: 99.4% model accuracy under 15% adversarial participation
Encrypted FedAvg in power-grid CPS: 94.3% prediction accuracy
Differential privacy on X-ray gradient updates (epsilon=2.0 Gaussian noise) [110]: ~80% diagnostic accuracy; up to ~12% accuracy degradation for epsilon<1.0
Pruned ResNet-50 in traffic management: 92% vehicle detection accuracy while cutting camera-to-cloud bandwidth by 68%
50-node cluster at 180 ms intervals: 94.7% prediction accuracy
Smart-grid FL: communication cost reduced by 55%; hierarchical structure reduces communication overhead by 10-20% vs. flat
Decentralized FL-CPS: collision false positives reduced 41% vs. centralized, at 12-18% higher energy cost
FL accuracy within delta<0.9% of centralized training while reducing data exposure by 97% [97]
EXAM COVID-19 model [152]: AUC>0.92 at 24/72 h post-ER, +16% AUC and +38% interpretability across collaborating institutions
Byzantine-robust FL with blockchain validation: ~92% attack detection, at 22% higher energy cost
baselines_compared: Prior surveys in Table I: FL-for-current-applications survey [17], and other FL surveys up to [18]; FL-for-IoT/IIoT surveys [19][20]; UAV-enabled FL survey [21] — the paper argues none covered FL-for-CPS specifically

Within cited works, common reference points are centralized/single-site training and non-FL collaborative learning (e.g., FL vs. whole-dataset training on BraTS; FL vs. generic non-personalized models)
